title: BBQ Baby Back Ribs
subtitle: Dry-rubbed pork ribs slow-baked until tender, finished with BBQ sauce
description: American BBQ baby back ribs without a smoker: a dry rub of smoked paprika, brown sugar, and spices applied the night before, then the ribs baked low and slow in the oven wrapped in foil for two hours, unwrapped and glazed with barbecue sauce for the final thirty minutes. The foil stage steams the ribs tender from the inside; the open stage caramelises the sauce into a sticky, dark glaze. The result is fall-from-the-bone ribs without specialist equipment.
sourceNotes: BBQ baby back ribs are a central dish of American barbecue culture, with regional variations across the Carolinas, Memphis, Kansas City, and Texas. The oven low-and-slow method with foil steaming is the standard home-cook adaptation of pit-smoked technique, described in numerous American barbecue cookbooks from the 1970s onward.

recipeTools.notes: Large enough to hold the rack of ribs flat. Two baking sheets if cooking a full rack.
Heavy-duty foil for wrapping the ribs — standard foil may tear during the steam phase.

glossaryTerms.definition: A blend of dry spices applied to raw meat before cooking. The rub penetrates the surface layer as the meat cooks, forming a crust called the bark.
A thin, silver-grey membrane on the bone side of a rack of ribs. Removing it before cooking allows the rub and smoke (or steam) to penetrate from both sides and makes the finished ribs easier to eat.

ingredients.notes: Splashed inside the foil packet to create steam

steps: Remove the membrane from the bone side of each rack: slide a butter knife under the silver-grey membrane at one end, grip it with a paper towel, and pull firmly — it should come off in one piece. This step makes the ribs much easier to eat.

Combine the dry rub ingredients. Pat the ribs dry and rub the spice mixture all over both sides, pressing it in firmly. Wrap the racks in cling film and refrigerate overnight, or for at least 2 hours.
When ready to cook, heat the oven to 150 °C fan (170 °C / gas 3).

Place each rack of ribs on a large piece of heavy-duty foil, bone-side up. Splash 2 tbsp of apple cider vinegar over each rack. Wrap the foil tightly around each rack to create a sealed parcel. Place on baking sheets.
Bake for 2 hours — the ribs steam inside the foil and become very tender.
Open the foil carefully — steam will escape. Brush the ribs generously with BBQ sauce on both sides.
Return the ribs to the oven, unwrapped, and bake at 200 °C fan (220 °C / gas 7) for 20–30 minutes, brushing with more sauce every 10 minutes, until the glaze is sticky and caramelised.
Rest for 10 minutes before cutting into individual ribs. Serve with extra BBQ sauce alongside.

content.text: from the bone side before applying the

. If left on, it becomes leathery during cooking and can prevent the rub from penetrating from the underside. The overnight rest in the rub is not essential but noticeably improves the depth of flavour.

Remove the membrane from {{baby-back-ribs}} of rib racks. Apply the combined dry rub all over and refrigerate for at least 2 hours. Heat the oven to 150 °C fan. Wrap each rack in foil with {{apple-cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar inside the packet. Bake for 2 hours.

Open the foil. Brush {{bbq-sauce}} of BBQ sauce generously over both sides. Return to the oven at 200 °C fan, unwrapped, for 20–30 minutes, brushing with more sauce every 10 minutes, until sticky and caramelised. Rest for 10 minutes, then cut into individual ribs and serve.
